name: Alton Baker Canal
description:
Alton Baker Pond and Canoe Canal are located within Alton Baker Park, one of the most popular and multi-functional parks in Eugene.
The park is located on the north bank of the Willamette River just east of the Ferry Street Bridge and Coburg Road. It is accessible by foot or bike from either side of the river via the River Bank Trails. By car you can enter via the Club Road underpass off Coburg Road or from Autzen Stadium off MLK/Centennial.
Located with in the park are two ponds and a canal, which can be accessed by rented canoes during the summer months.

name: Ana Reservoir
description:
This is a 60-acrereservoir located about 23 miles northeast of Paisley, Oregon. This reservoirmaintains a temperature of about 58 degrees and is open year around. It isstocked with hybrid bass and rainbow trout. Boats can be launched from animproved ramp and an accessible dock is available. Many anglers target hybridbass January to March using crankbaits (e.g. Rapala’s) from the shore, butthese fish can be caught anytime during the year. Anglers using boats can trollcrankbaits or jig for hybrid bass. Check the regulations for special conditionsthat exist for harvesting hybrid bass. Trout fishing can be good anytime of theyear because water temperatures keep them active. Bait, lures, flies, ortrolling will be productive for trout.

Fish species: hybrid (white/striped) bass, rainbow trout, chub

ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/southeast-zone/)
Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)
Fishing Regulations (http://www.eregulations.com/oregon/fishing/)

name: Ana River
description:
This is a spring-fed river that begins at the Ana Reservoir dam and continues through Summer Lake Wildlife Area (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/visitors/summer_lake_wildlife_area.asp). Sampling has identified substantially higher numbers of trout in the first 5.5 miles or the river. The water temperature remains about 58 degrees and is optimal for rainbow trout. Bait can be used on this river. Bait, lures, and flies are all effective methods for catching rainbow trout.


Fish species: rainbow trout, chub

name: Antelope Flat Reservoir
description:
This is a 170-acre reservoir located in the Maury Mountain area of the Ochoco National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gjAwhwtDDw9_AI8zPyhQoY6BdkOyoCAGixyPg!/?ss=110601&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&cid=fsbdev3_036040&navid=091000000000000&pnavid=null&position=Welcome.Html&ttype=detail&pname=Deschutes%20&%20Ochoco%20National%20Forests%20&%20Crooked%20River%20National%20Grassland-%20Home), 30 miles southeast of Prineville. Antelope Flat Reservoir Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recarea/?recid=38784) and boat ramp are located at the west end of the reservoir. This a very productive reservoir with excellent opportunities for rainbow trout 14-18 inches. Flies, lures and bait are all effective. Bank angling opportunities are present, however, anglers in boats or float tubes tend to be more successful.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Applegate Reservoir
description:
This is a 988-acre reservoir in southwestern Oregon just north of the California border. The lake was created in 1980 by damming the mainstream of the Applegate River 47 miles upstream from its confluence with the Rogue. It is named after Lindsay Applegate, a prominent early Oregon pioneer. This lake is located in the Skskiyou Mountains within the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/rogue-siskiyou/). This is a flood control reservoir and depth varies dramatically between spring and fall.


The lake is popular for cycling, boating, canoeing, fishing and hiking the various trails near the lake. The elevation of the lake is 1,987 feet. Camping: Camping is available at Hart-Tish (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/rogue-siskiyou/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea?recid=70232&actid=29),and Watkins (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/rogue-siskiyou/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea?recid=70404&actid=29) campgrounds. Dispersed hike-in camping is also available around the reservoir at French Gulch, Latgawa Cove, Streamtown, Harr Point and Tipsu Tyee campgrounds. Seattle Bar is a day-use only area at the south end of the reservoir. Jackson Picnic Park and Flumet Flat campgrounds are located about 5.6 miles downstream from the reservoir. Fees at some locations. Private concessionaire available seasonally.

Boaters are subject to a 10 m.p.h. speed limit.

Fish species: trout, chinook slamon, largemouth bass, smallmouth bass, crappie


ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/southwest-zone/)
Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)

description:

It is stocked with catchable and trophy trout in the spring and early summer. Fishing can be good all summer, depending on water conditions and stocking level. The pond has good bank access and a great place for kids to fish. The pond is managed for youths 17 and under.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Ben Irving Reservoir
description:
This is a 250-acre lake in Douglas County, Oregon, created by the construction of Berry Creek Dam, which impounds Berry Creek. The reservoir is named for a longtime Oregon outdoorsman and is located in a narrow canyon about 10 miles southwest of Winston, Oregon. Boats are allowed on the reservoir, which is popular for water skiing, butthere is an electric motor only section for angling.

Amenities: boat ramp, picnic tables, barbecue, restroom

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, bluegill, yellow perch, black crappie, brown bullhead

name: Beulah Reservoir
description:
This is a 2,060-acre irrigation reservoir located on the North Fork Malheur River 19 miles north of Juntura, Oregon. It is open year round to trout fishing. The reservoir supports populations of bull trout, redband trout, and whitefish. The resevoir is stocked in the spring when enough water is present to support trout into the fall. Camping sites exist at the boat ramp and along the eastern shore. Best fishing occurs in the spring from ice out until June. Fishing improves again in the fall after water temperatures cool

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Big Cultus Lake
description:
This is a 785-acre lake located about 50 miles south of Bend, Oregon in the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon). This lake is deep and holds lake trout in addition to stocked rainbows. There is a resort (http://www.cultuslakeresort.com/) on the southeast shore of the lake, with restaurant, boat and motor rentals, cabins, supplies and gas. Cultus Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38836&actid=29) is a large campground with sites for RVs up to 30 feet. For campers who want more privacy West Cultus Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39028&actid=29) offers boat-in only campsites. In addition to fishing, activities at Big Cultus Lake include swimming, water skiing and hiking.

Fish species: lake trout, stocked rainbow trout, brook trout

name: Big Lava Lake
description:
This is a 160-acre lake located within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) approximately 38 miles south of Bend, Oregon. This beautiful spring-fed lake reaches a maximum depth of about 30 feet and supports populations of brook trout and rainbow trout. Camping and a boat ramp are available at Lava Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38894&actid=29) on the south shore. There is also a private resort with RV hook-ups, boat rentals and supplies. Boats are subject to a 10 m.p.h. speed limit.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout.

name: Big Rock Reservoir
description:
This is a 5-acre reservoir located 15 north of Plush, Oregon within the Bureau of Land Management's Lakeview, Oregon District (http://www.blm.gov/or/districts/lakeview/index.php). This reservoir is stocked with fingerling rainbow trout annually and has the capacity to grow fish up to 16”. The reservoir can summer kill (algae bloom) occasionally as happened in 2011. The reservoir was re-stocked in 2012. This is a great little reservoir to float tube and the fish are very willing to bite because they don’t get much fishing pressure. The reservoir does not show up on current BLM maps, but is located in Mary Ann Draw. Use BLM Road #6105 to access Mary Ann Draw.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Blue Lake
description:

The lake is accessed via a 3-mile trail off of NF-3372. The trailhead is about equal distances from Bly, Paisley, or Lakeview. This lake makes a great day trip or overnight camping excursion. Fingerling rainbow trout are stocked annually with mules and help from the High Desert Trail Riders. Bait, flies, and lures all work well for trout from 6”-16”.

The lake is stocked annually with legal- and trophy-sizedrainbow trout. Small boats can be launched from a boat ramp at Campbell Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59755&actid=29) but are restricted toelectric motors and a 5 m.p.h. speed limit. Camping is also available nearby atDead Horse Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59761&actid=29).

name: Chewaucan River
description:
The river has mixed private-U.S. Forest Service ownership but has many public access points on the tributaries and mainstem river. The river is managed for native redband trout and has multiple campgrounds to stage from. Special harvest and gear regulations are in effect on the river. Native redband trout range from 10-20+ inches. The lower river (below Hwy 31) has largemouth bass near Rivers End Reservoir, a private reservoir. Most bass are 6”-10” long, and brown bullhead catfish are also present, but small.

Fish species: rainbow trout, redband trout, bass, catfish

It is heavily stocked with fingerling trout in the spring and is known for producing some large rainbows. There is a boat ramp, and many anglers troll for trout while others fish from the shore. There is a boat ramp on the southeast end of the reservoir, accessed by a gravel road.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Cleawox Lake
description:
Cleawox lake is 87 acres in size and is one of the most popular lakes on the Oregon Coast. Its defining features are the large sand dunes that form its southern and western shores. From these dunes one can gain access to the Oregon Dunes National Recreation Area. Most of the shoreline is in public ownership with easy bank access. The elevation is near sea level.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, crappie, some yellow perch, bullhead, bluegill

name: Cottage Grove Reservoir
description:
A 1.150-acre reservoir located six miles south of Cottage Grove, Oregon. This reservoir has four day-use only parks, three on the lakeshore and one on the river immediately below the dam. All are open mid-May to mid-September.

Lakeside Park is located on London Road just past the dam. The park has paved roads and parking lots, flush toilets, picnic tables, fire rings and a year-around boat ramp.

Wilson Creek Park is on Reservoir Road at the southwest end of the lake, and has paved roads and parking lots, flush toilets, picnic tables, fire rings, a children's play area, unsupervised swim area and courtesy boat dock.

Riverside Park is on the east bank of the river, downstream from the dam. This primitive park has a gravel road and parking area, two picnic tables and a vault toilet. A paved path with pull-outs suitable for wheelchairs is close enough to the river for fishing.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, brown bullhead catfish

Trout Stocking Schedule - https://myodfw.com/fishing/species/trout/stocking-schedule?field_zone_value=All&field_waterbody_name_value_1=COTTAGE+GROVE+RES&field_planned_stocking_date_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-01-01&field_planned_stocking_date_end_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-12-31

name: Crane Prairie Reservoir
description:
This is a 5-square-mile reservoir located in the heart of one of Oregon's richest wildlife areas. The reservoir ranges from 10-20 feet deep and is a premier fishery for large rainbow trout and brook trout. Largemouth bass and kokanee are also available.

Hatchery released rainbow trout are all marked with clipped adipose fins. Harvest is limited to five trout per day with only one fish over 20 inches, wild rainbow trout must be released.

Large numbers of brook trout are available, with the best fishing early and late in the season. Fish for brook trout at dawn and dusk with flies, lures and bait. Anglers after bigger brook trout should cast their fly, lure or bait close to cover such as submerged logs or undercut banks.

Crane Prairie offers fair to good largemouth bass fishing opportunities, which improve as water temperature increase. These fish typically range from 2-7 pounds.

There are good boat ramps at Rock Creek, Quinn River Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTJw8jAwjQL8h2VAQAzHJMsQ!!?ss=110601&ttype=recarea&recid=38958&actid=29&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110000000000000&pnavid=&cid=null&pname=Central+Oregon+-+Quinn+River+Campground) and Crane Prairie Resort (http://www.crane-prairie-resort-guides.com/). The resort offers lodging, meals and supplies. Motorized boats are allowed but are restricted to 10 m.p.h.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout, kokanee, largemouth bass, crappie

name: Crescent Lake
description:
This is a 6-square-mile lake located within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) about 75 miles south of Eugene, Oregon. Open year-around, this large, deep lake produces lake trout ranging from 5 to 20 pounds.Crescent Lake also has a good kokanee fishery, producing fish in the 12-13" range. The lake is regularly stocked with brown and rainbow trout.

Camping is available aty Crescent Lake (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39060&actid=29), Spring (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39102&actid=29) and Contorta Point (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39052&actid=29) campgrounds. Picnic facilities are available at Simax Beach, Tandy Bay and Tranquil Cove. Cabins are available for rent at Crescent Lake Resort (http://crescentlakeresort.com/), which also has a restarant, store, and boat rentals.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, lake trout, kokanee

name: Delintment Reservoir
description:
This is a 50-acre reservoir located in the Ochoco National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) approximately 47 miles northwest of Burns, Oregon. This lake is situated within a ponderosa pine forest and offers good bank access. Boats are allowed on the reservoir but are restricted to a 5 m.p.h. speed limit. Fishing is best in the spring and early summer, as the reservoir can become choked with aquatic vegetation in the summer.

A large campground with overnight camping, day-use area, boat lauch and accessible boat dock is located on-site.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Detroit Lake - Hoover Campground
description:
Hoover Campground rests at an elevation of 1,600 feet on the shores of Detroit Lake. Camp sites are surrounded by towering trees, making for secluded camping spots.

There are several hiking trails in the area, including Stahlman Point Trail, which offers beautiful vistas of the lake and surrounding mountain area. There is a boat ramp adjacent to the campground . Additional amenities include: ADA fishing platform, camp sites, vault toilet, grills, paved parking lot.

Reservations are available  May through September.

Fish species: rainbow trout

Trout Stocking Schedule -https://myodfw.com/fishing/species/trout/stocking-schedule?field_zone_value=All&field_waterbody_name_value_1=DETROIT+RES&field_planned_stocking_date_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-01-01&field_planned_stocking_date_end_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-12-31

name: Detroit Lake - Kane's Marina
description:
Kane’s Marina is located on Detroit Lake 50 miles east of Salem. Detroit Lake is a prime recreation area easily accessible from both eastern Oregon and the Willamette Valley. At 1,600 feet above sea level, Detroit Lake is fed by clear mountain water that warms to perfect temperature for swimming, skiing, wakeboarding, tubing and other in-the-water activities during the summer months.

Kane’s Marina (http://www.kanesmarina.com/) is a private facility that offers 400 slips for seasonal and overnight moorage, 200 ft. of guest tie-up, seasonal and overnight RV spaces, boat rentals, fuel, and convenience store where fishing licenses and tackle are sold.

Fish species: rainbow trout
Trout Stocking Schedule -https://myodfw.com/fishing/species/trout/stocking-schedule?field_zone_value=All&field_waterbody_name_value_1=DETROIT+RES&field_planned_stocking_date_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-01-01&field_planned_stocking_date_end_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-12-31

name: Detroit Lake - Mongold Day Use Area
description:
Mongold Day Use Area (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_91.php) is 1.5 miles west of Detroit Lake State Park. It's the only public boat launch facility on the lake featuring a swimming area, grass beach, picnic facilities and restrooms. Mongold is a year-round fee area.

Mongold has 58 single car parking spaces and three fully-accessible parking spaces near the swimming area, and another 25 single car parking spaces at the top of the parking lot. There are 120 vehicle/trailer parking spaces, and three that are accessible to people with disabilities.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Detroit Lake State Park
description:
Located in the Cascade Mountains, Detroit Lake State Park is the gateway to beautiful Detroit Lake. The 400-foot-deep lake was created in 1953 when the U.S. Army Corp of Engineers completed the Detroit Dam project. The lake is over nine miles long with more than 32 miles of shore line. It is popular for fishing, boating, swimming, water skiing, and personal watercraft.

The park has a large campground bordering the lake. Along with two swimming areas, there are two playgrounds, a wildlife viewing area, and a visitor center with historical exhibits and convenience store.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Devil's Lake
description:
Devils Lake is a 680-acre lake located located on the central Oregon Coast by Lincoln City. The lake connects to the Pacific Ocean by the D River, once considered the shortest in the world. To the east of the lake is the Siuslaw National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/siuslaw).

This lake is appr. 3 miles long and 1/2 mile wide, with an average depth of 10 feet and a maximum depth of 22 feet.

Fish species: rainbow trout


Trout Stocking Schedule -https://myodfw.com/fishing/species/trout/stocking-schedule?field_zone_value=3&field_waterbody_name_value_1=dev&field_planned_stocking_date_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-01-01&field_planned_stocking_date_end_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-12-31

name: Devils Lake
description:
Devils Lake is a beautiful 23-acre shallow lake located adjacent to Century Drive about 30 miles southwest of Bend, Oregon.The exquisite turquoise color of the water coupled with the scenic views makes this lake a delightful place to visit. The easy access, fishable shoreline and stocking of catchable rainbows make Devils Lake an excellent place to take children fishing. In addition to the stocked rainbows there is a natural reproducing population of brook trout.

Devils Lake is a shallow lake with an average depth of just over three feet and a maximum depth of 10 feet. Motorized boats are prohibited on this body of water.

Small boats or rafts can be launched near the campground.

Fish species: rainbow trout


ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/southeast-zone/)

name: Dexter Reservoir
description:
Situated 15 miles east of Eugene in the town of Lowell, Dexter Reservoir is the last impoundment on the Middle Fork of the Willamette before it joins the Coast Fork to create the main stem of the Willamette. Steady water levels make Dexter a reliable choice for first-rate boating throughout the year. At 1,025 acres, Dexter is 3.3 miles long and a mile wide at it widest point; it’s regularly stocked with rainbow trout.

Fish species: rainbow trout


Dexter State Recreation Area (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_244.php

name: Diamond Lake
description:
Diamond Lake is a 2,824-acre lake within the Umpqua National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/umpqua) in southern Oregon, just north of Crater Lake National Park (http://www.nps.gov/crla/index.htm) in Douglas County. This lake is one of the most popular recreational fisheries in southern Oregon. Fishing is done both from boats and from the bank. There are five paved boat ramps, three U.S. Forest Service campgrounds and a privately owned resort around Diamond Lake.

Campgrounds: Diamond Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63480), Broken Arrow Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63460), Thielson View Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63618)

Fish species: rainbow trout

ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/southwest-zone/)

Fishing Regulations (http://www.eregulations.com/oregon/fishing/)

name: Directions from Koko Lake to Booth Lake
Dog Lake
description:
This is 51- acre reservoir with a 30’ maximum depth. The lake is located on the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema). The lake contains largemouth bass, yellow perch, crappie, bluegill, brown bullhead, chub, and redband trout. Redband trout must be immediately released. The reservoir has two campgrounds, one on the lake and one about ½ mile from the lake. A boat ramp and pit toilets are available.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Dorena Reservoir
description:
This 1,840-acre reservoir is located about six miles east of Cottage Grove, Ore. The reservoir is located in rolling, partially wooded hill country. Its three parks offer boating, swimming, sailing, fishing and water skiing. Schwarz Park, located downstream of the dam, offers overnight camping for families and groups. Overnight camping is also available at Baker Park on the south side of the lake. The lakeshore is managed for wildlife habitat as well, including many species of birds and animals.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, brown bullhead catfish, and white crappie

description:
This is a 33-acre reservoir 5 miles southwest of Silver Lake, Oregon. The reservoir is stocked annually with fingerling, catchable, and trophy rainbow trout. There is an 8-unit campground with pit toilet and a boat ramp. Fishing is good using bait, lures, and files. Fly fishing is better in the morning because this reservoir can be windy in the afternoon.


Fish species: rainbow trout

name: East Lake
description:
This is a 1,050-acre lake located within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) appr. 35 miles southwest of Bend. This popular recreation lake is situated in a setting of wooded shorelines, sandy beaches and brilliant blue-green water. The lake is one of two trout lakes nestled in the caldera of the Newberry National Volcanic Monument (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recarea/?recid=66159), the largest ice-age volcano in Oregon.

In addition to fishing for kokanee, rainbow trout and brown trout, people come to this lake to picnic and camp. East Lake shares this outstanding geologic area with Paulina Lake and both lakes are well known for their large brown trout. East Lake is known for producing brown trout over 10 pounds. Anglers also like to target rainbow trout. It is a popular fly angling destination

East Lake is 6,381 feet above sea level, is approximately a mile in diameter, and the average depth is just over 67 feet.

Motorized boats are allowed on this lake and can launch at any of three locations - Cinder Hill Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38324&actid=29), Hot Springs Boating Site (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTJw8jAwjQL8h2VAQAzHJMsQ!!?ss=110601&ttype=recarea&recid=38378&actid=78&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110000000000000&pnavid=&cid=null&pname=Newberry+National+Volcanic+Monument+-+Deschutes+NF+-+Hot+Springs+Boating+Site) and East Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38346&actid=29). Supplies are available at the East Lake Resort and RV Park.

Fish species: brown trout, rainbow trout, kokanee


ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/central-zone/)
Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)
Fishing Regulations (http://www.eregulations.com/oregon/fishing/)

description:

name: Elbow Lake
description:
Elbow Lake is a scenic gem of a lake visible from Hwy. 101. It is easily accessible and is equipped with a small boat ramp. The brushy shoreline makes bank angling difficult but rafts, float tubes and cartop boats are popular. Camping is located nearby at Tahkenitch Lake. Tahkenitch Landing Campground (https://fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTJw8jAwjQL8h2VAQAzHJMsQ!!/?ss=110612&ttype=recarea&recid=42587&actid=42&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110000000000000&pnavid=&cid=null&pname=Oregon+Dunes+National+Recreation+Area+-+Tahkenitch+Landing+Campground) has 36 tent/trailer sites, water, and flush toilets.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, perch

name: Elk Lake
description:
This is a 250-acre lake in Deschutes County located approximately 32 miles southwest of Bend, Oregon. This picturesque lake offers sweeping views of Central Oregon's Three Sisters mountains as well as Mt. Bachelor. With depths of 25 to 65 ft., Elk Lake supports populations of kokanee and brook trout and cutthroat trout.

Camping and boat ramps are located at Elk Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c5/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTSw8jAwgAykeaxcN4jhYG_h4eYX5hPgYwefy6w0H24dcPNgEHcDTQ9_PIz03VL8iNMMgycVQEAIzTHkw!/dl3/d3/L2dJQSEvUUt3QS9ZQnZ3LzZfME80MEkxVkFCOTBFMktTNUJIMjAwMDAwMDA!/?ss=110601&ttype=recarea&recid=38860&actid=29&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110130000000000&pnavid=110000000000000&cid=FSE_003705&pname=Central+Oregon+-+Elk+Lake+Campground), Point Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38950&actid=29) and Little Fawn Boating Site.. A fourth boat ramp is available at Elk Lake Resort (http://www.elklakeresort.net/), on the west shore of the lake.

Fish species: brook trout, cutthroat trout, kokanee


ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/central-zone/)

name: Emigrant Lake
description:
Emigrant Lake is one of the most popular recreational destinations in Jackson County, Oregon. This lake has numerous amenities including developed campgrounds and picnic areas, two boat ramps, restrooms with showers. Reservations are recommended to camp at Emigrant Lake County Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3525).

Caution: This reservoir is listed under a health advisory for high levels of mercury. The health advisory suggests that anglers only eat trout stocked in the reservoir because they have not had as much time to absorb mercury as fish that spend their whole life in the reservoir.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, smallmouth bass, black crappie

name: Fall River
description:
The Fall River is a 12-mile long tributary of the Deschutes River. An ODFW fish hatchery is situated next to the river near its source about 25 miles south of Bend, Oregon and is open from 8 a.m. to 6 p.m.

Some catchable trout raised at the hatchery are released into the river. The hatchery grounds are open to the public. Limited to fly angling only.

Fish species: rainbow trout

description:

name: Fish Lake - Rogue Watershed
description:
Fish Lake is a 483-acre lake in southern Oregon about 35 miles northeast of Medford.

The lake has a maximum depth of 31 feet and an average depth of 18 feet. The lake is located within the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/rogue-siskiyou/). Camping is available nearby at Fish Lake (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/rogue-siskiyou/recreation/recarea/?recid=69728), North Fork (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/rogue-siskiyou/recreation/recarea/?recid=69832) and Doe Point (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/rogue-siskiyou/recreation/recarea/?recid=69716) campgrounds. Popular activities include swimming, boating, hiking horseback riding, hunting, fishing and wildlife viewing. This is one of only two lakes in Oregon where tiger trout are being stocked on an experimental basis. Fish Lake Resort (http://www.fishlakeresort.net/) offers rental cabins, RV sites, primitive campsites, boat rentals and moorage. Boaters are subject to a 10 m.p.h. speed limit.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout, tiger trout, spring chinook

name: Floras Lake
description:
This is a 250-acre lake located about 17 miles south of Bandon, Ore. The lake has a healthy population of cutthroat trout and some largemouth bass. The lake is also stocked with catchable and trophy trout in the spring. Access to the lake is mainly by boat as bank access is limited. This lake reaches a maximum depth of about 50 feet. Boice-Cope County Park (http://www.co.curry.or.us/parks/parks.htm) provides camping and a boat ramp.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, largemouth bass

name: Fourmile Lake
description:
This is a 740-acre lake located within the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) approximately 40 miles west of Klamath Falls, Oregon. The lake access is west of Lake of the Woods and requires driving down a dirt road for six miles. This lake is at an elevation of 5,800 feet, and the access road is usually open by the Fourth of July, although the lake is open to fishing year-round. Fourmile Lake is a deep, clear lake with a maximum depth of 175 feet. Water levels fluctuate widely as 15,000 acre feet of water is dedicated for irrigating the Rogue Valley.

Fourmile Lake is generally stocked with legal- to trophy-sized rainbow trout in late June through August. Lake trout reaching 10 pounds are also available. Kokanee are rare as lake trout have reduced their numbers but average 14 inches. Brook trout are common but difficult to catch. Lake trout, brook trout, and kokanee are all self-sustaining populations. Only rainbow trout are stocked in the lake. Look for caddisfly hatches in July and termite hatches in October. Mosquitoes can be quite bad in the summer.

Camping is available near the shoreline at Fourmile Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59727&actid=29), which is located at the foot of Mt. McLaughlin. The campground offers picnic sites, accessible tent and trailer campsites, drinking water, restrooms and an unimproved boat ramp. The Sky Lakes Wilderness (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/recarea/?recid=60187) encompasses most of Fourmile Lake. Several trails lead to high lakes that are stocked every other year with brook trout. The most productive high lake is Badger Lake, which is approximately one mile north of Fourmile Lake. Other lakes within hiking distance that have fish include Squaw, Woodpecker and Long lakes.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout, kokanee, lake trout

Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)

name: Galesville Reservoir
description:
This 630-acre reservoir is open to angling year-round. In addition to trout, the reservoir is sometimes stocked with coho salmon smolts. Many people mistakenly think these fish are kokanee. All of the smolts are adipose fin-clipped. In this reservoir, all landlocked salmon are considered trout and are part of the five-per-day bag limit. This reservoir has a new campground in addition to the picnic area, plus two boat ramps.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, bluegill, brown bullhead, black crappie, cutthroat trout

name: Garrison Lake
description:
This is a 134-acre lake located in Port Orford, Ore. It is stocked several times in the spring with trophy and catchable trout. Fishing can be good through May but warmer water and aquatic weeds make for tough fishing through summer. The best way to fish the lake is by boat but bank fishing can be good of the fishing pier on 12th street. Boat ramps are located at 12th Street and Pinehurst Roads. Anglers should keep an eye on the weather and target the lake when winds are light.
Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, largemouth bass

name: Haystack Reservoir
description:
This is a large reservoir located in the Crooked River National Grassland (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTJw8jAwjQL8h2VAQAzHJMsQ!!/?ss=110601&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110130000000000&pnavid=110000000000000&recid=38274&groupid=null&ttype=recarea&pname=Deschutes) about 9 miles south of Madras, Ore. This is an irrigation reservoir, so water levels can vary substantially seasonally and daily.

Haystack Reservoir is open year-round supports a diverse fish population. Legal-sized rainbow trout are stocked in mid-April. Moderate numbers of large brown trout and kokanee are also present, as are several species of warmwater fish.

Boat ramps and camping areas are available on the east and west shores.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, kokanee, crappie, largemouth bass, brown bullhead

name: Heart Lake
description:
This is a 19-acre lake located on the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) in a secluded forest setting at an elevation of 5,700 feet. Access is by Forest Road #3715, which is paved but narrow. Boats are allowed on this lake but are restricted to electric motors and subject to a 5 m.p.h. speed limit.

Kokanee and rainbow trout are stocked in the lake. Kokanee fishing can be very good in the spring and early summer. Catch rates on rainbow is generally good throughout the summer. Fat head minnow occur in the lake so lures and flies that mimic this food can be effective. Illegally introduced brown bullherad catfish were recently found. There is no limit on catfish; keep as many as you want.

This is a day-use only lake. An improved boat ramp, two picnic tables and primitive restrooms are available at the lake. Camping is available nearby at Lofton Reservoir Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59711&actid=29).

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown bullhead

name: Hebo Lake
description:
Hebo Lake is about two acres in size and is fairly shallow. A gravel trail around the lake provides good bank access. There are four ADA barrier-free fishing platforms; two are accessible from the paved road. Only non-motorized boats are permitted on the lake, which is better suited to canoes, small rafts or float tubes. The lake is located near several popular hiking trails.

Amenities: Day use area with picnic tables and restrooms, campground with 15 sites.

Nearby state parks: Bob Straub State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_183.php), Cape Kiwanda State Natural Area (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_180.php), Clay Myers State Natural Area (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_248.php).

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Hosmer Lake
description:
This is a 112-acre lake located about 37 miles southwest of Bend, Oregon. It is well known for its breathtaking views of Mt. Bachelor, South Sisters, and Broken Top, as well as Atlantic salmon, brook trout and rainbow trout.

Deep snow is typical during the winter, limiting road access to June through October. Boating is allowed but is restricted to electric motors but not when fishing. Angling is restricted to fly fishing, and float tubes are popular for due to limited backcasting space. Hosmer is a popular canoeing and paddleboarding destination.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout, cutthroat trout, Atlantic salmon

name: Howard Prairie Reservoir
description:
Howard Prairie Lake is a popular destination in sourthern Oregon that offers an abundance of recreational opportunities, including excellent trout and bass fishing. This 1,990-acre reservoir is located about 43 miles south of Medford and 24 miles east of Ashland. It has a maximum depth of 80 feet and an average depth of 35 feet. The lake is formed by the 100-foot tall Howard Prairie Dam, which impounds Beaver Creek, in the Klamath River watershed.

Amenities: Asperkaha Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3535), Grizzly County Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3536), Klum Landing County Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3537), Lily Glen County Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3538), Sugar Pine County Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3539), Willow Point County Park (http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3529), The Resort at Howard Prairie Lake

Fish species: rainbow trout, bass

name: Kingsley Reservoir
description:
This is a 60-acre lake located about 11 miles southwest of Hood River, Oregon. It is also known as Green Point Reservoir. There is a small campground located at the north end of the lake. Boats are allowed. Bank anglers can fish from a gently sloping dam at the north end of the lake as well as other locations.

Anglers should focus efforts early in the year, as the reservoir level will drop rapidly as the season progresses. This receiver sometimes receives excess adult hatchery steelhead from Hood River, when available.

Fish species: rainbow trout, excess steelhead (when available)

name: Lake Billy Chinook
description:
This is a large reservoir about 8 miles southwest of Madras, Oregon created by the impoundment of the Deschutes River behind Round Butte Dam.

Lake Billy Chinook is one of Central Oregon's most popular recreational sites. The reservoir supports populations of kokanee, bull trout, and smallmouth and largemouth bass. In addition to fishing, swimming, water skiing and houseboat rentals are popular activities.

A boat ramp and camping are available at Cove Palisades State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_32.php). A boat ramp, boat moorage, restaurant, and boat and houseboat rentals are available at Cove Palisades Resort and Marina (http://www.covepalisadesresort.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=12&Itemid=20).

Fish species: bull trout, redband trout, brown trout, kokanee, largemouth bass, smallmouth bass

name: Lake of the Woods
description:
This is a 1,146-acre lake located within the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) between Klamath Falls and Medford, Oregon. This lake is located at an elevation of 4,949 feet above sea level and supports a wide range of recreational activities, including fishing, swimming, water skiing, picnicking, hiking, mountain biking and wildlife viewing. Water quality is very good with depths ranging from an average of 27 to 55 feet. It supports a diverse population of fish.

Camping is available at Aspen Point Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59725&actid=29) on the northeast shore of the lake and at Sunset Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTJw8jAwjQL8h2VAQAzHJMsQ!!/?ss=110602&ttype=recarea&recid=59731&actid=79&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110400000000000&pnavid=110000000000000&cid=null&pname=Fremont-Winema+National+Forest+-+Sunset+Campground+at+Lake+of+the+Woods) on the west side of the lake. Amenities include piped water, flush toilets, RV dump station, accessible restrooms, boat launch and pier. A day use fee of five dollars is required at all campgrounds and boat ramps. Lodging is also available at Lake of the Woods Lodge, which offers cabins, a nice restaurant, store and gas. For more information, visit the Lake of the Woods website (http://www.lakeofthewoodsresort.com).

The most popular fishing is for hatchery rainbow trout in the spring by boat. The most effective method is to troll deep to catch hatchery rainbow trout.

Largemouth bass fishing is popular with small tournaments occurring every year. Look for largemouth bass around fallen trees, the many docks that occur in the lake, and around emergent vegetation such as lily pads.

Small yellow perch are the most abundant fish in the lake and can be exciting fishing for youth. Small spinners or bait work well for yellow perch. Ice fishing for these fish is popular.

A good population of brown bullhead occurs in the lake. A worm or other stink bait fished on the bottom work well for brown bullhead. Look for areas of submergent vegetation. North of Aspen Point Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59725&actid=29) has abundant brown bullhead.

Fishing at night is allowed for trout to target the nocturnal brown trout. Trophy brown trout occur in the lake and fishing on the east side of the lake is most productive for brown trout. Try using flourescent colored lures in late evening near the Boy Scout Camp.

Hardy anglers can hike into the Mountain Lake Wilderness (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/recarea/?recid=60185) and fish for brook trout and rainbow trout in Weston Lake, Como Lake Harriette, Paragon and Mystic. The Pacific Crest Trail (http://www.fs.usda.gov/pct/) is nearby.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout, brown trout, kokanee, black crappie, brown bullhead, yellow perch largemouth bass, smallmouth bass, tui chub

name: Laurance Lake
description:
This is a 104-acre irrigation reservoir located on the Mt. Hood National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/mthood) about 23 miles southwest of Hood River, Oregon. Water levels fluctuate with irrigation draws so best to check ahead before heading out to this reservoir. There is a primitive campground and boat ramp at the south end of the reservoir, just off of Laurance Lake Rd.

This reservoir is open from late April through October and is stocked with catchable rainbow trout. It also supports a small population of bull trout. Only adipose fin-clipped trout may be retained.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, brown trout

name: Lemolo Reservoir - Umpqua
description:
This is a 415-acre reservoir in the North Umpqua River basin. Three boat ramps, four campgrounds and resort are located on the lake. Lemolo Lake Resort (http://www.lemololakeresort.com/) offers gas, food, lodging, bait, tackle, boat rentals and launching. Spectacular views of the High Cascade Mountains can be seen from the lake. In the winter there is cross-country skiing and a wealth of snowmobile trails that lead through sparkling landscapes.

Several Forest Service campgrounds are located in close proximity to the reservoir: Bunker Hill Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63466), East Lemolo Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63492), Inlet Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63508), Poole Creek Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/recarea?recid=63570)

name: Lofton Reservoir
description:
This is a 42-acre reservoir located in a forest setting at an elevation of 6,100 ft. within the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) approximately 39 miles northwest of Lakeview, Oregon. This reservoir is stocked annually with fingerlings, legal-sized and trophy rainbow trout. Fishing is generally best in the spring.
Look for brownrecreation signs on highway 140 directing people to “Lofton Recreation Area”. Itis about 9 miles from highway 140 to Lofton Reservoir. Picnic tables, primitive restrooms, campsites, and primitive restrooms are available on-site at the lake at Lofton Reservoir Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59711&actid=29).

Fish species: rainbow trout, tui chub

name: Loon Lake
description:
This is a 294-acre lake located in Douglas County about 15 miles southeast of Reedsport, Ore. There are two resorts and a BLM campground (http://www.blm.gov/or/resources/recreation/site_info.php?siteid=34) with a boat ramp on the north end of the lake. This lake is two miles long and reaches a maximum debth of 105 feet. Boats are allowed and this lake is popular for water skiing. However, it has a speed limit zone for fishing on the south end. It has both BLM and private resort campgrounds and boat ramps.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, largemouth bass, black crappie, bluegill, brown bullhead

name: Lost Creek Reservoir
description:
This is a 3,500-acre impoundment on the Rogue River above Shady Cove, Ore. It is deep throughout, reaching a maximum depth of 322 feet. Camping and a boat ramp is available at Stewart State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_30.php) on the south shore. A second boat ramp is located next to the dam on the north end of the lake.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, brown trout, spring chinook, largemouth bass, smallmouth bass, bluegill, crappie, pumpkinseed sunfish, brown bullhead

name: Lost Lake - (Mt. Hood)
description:
This is a 231-acre lake located on the north slope of Mt. Hood within the Mt. Hood National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/mthood) about 25 miles southwest of Hood River, Oregon. It can be reached from Lost Lake Road (Forest Rd. 13) This lake is open year-round and provides excellent fishing opportunity throught the summer. The picturesque lake also offers excellent boating, although the use of motors is prohibited. Access is generally from when the snow recedes in May through late fall.

This lake is stocked with legal- and trophy-sized rainbow trout. It also supports small populations brown and brook trout as well as kokanee.

Lost Lake Resort (http://lostlakeresort.org/lost/index.html) at the north end of the lake has a boat ramp, boat rentals. cabins and tent and RV sites. Camping is also available on the east side of the lake at Lost Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/mthood/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=53228&actid=29).

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, kokanee


ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/central-zone/)
Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)

name: Lost Lake - Clatsop County
description:
Lost Lake is located in a forested setting in the Coast Range Mountains west of Hwy. 26 in the Nehalem River basin. The lake is relatively small, about 15 acres. Some camping is available, but no improved facilities exist. The lake offers public bank access around the entire lake. A boat launch is available for small boats.

Alternate Activities: Good opportunities for wildlife viewing, particularly deer and elk. The Nehalem River is nearby, offering steelhead fishing in the spring and sea-run cutthroat fishing in the summer. Nearby state parks: Nehalem Bay State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_201.php), Oswald West State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_195.php).

Fish species: rainbow trout, surplus hatchery steelhead when available

Trout Stocking Schedule - https://myodfw.com/fishing/species/trout/stocking-schedule?field_zone_value=All&field_waterbody_name_value_1=LOST+LK&field_planned_stocking_date_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-01-01&field_planned_stocking_date_end_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-12-31

name: Lucky Reservoir
description:
This is a 6-acre reservoir located within the Bureau of Land Management's Lakeview, Oregon District (http://www.blm.gov/or/districts/lakeview/index.php). The reservoir is stocked with fingerling rainbow trout annually. The reservoir is muddy all year and anglers should target areas along the margin of the reservoir to catch fish. Access to the reservoir is through private property with an easement. Please leave gates how you find them.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Maidu Lake
description:
This is a 20-acre hike-in lake located approximately 38 miles west of LaPine, Ore., at the head of the North Umpqua River. It is usually accessible by June. It is a 7-mile hike from the Kelsey Valley horse camp side and approximately 3 miles from the Miller Lake side. https://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/umpqua/recreation/hiking/recarea/?recid=63738&actid=51. The lake is annually stocked with fingerling "FishWich" Rainbow Trout.

Fish species: brook trout, Rainbow Trout

name: Malheur Reservoir
description:
This is a 1,200-acre irrigation reservoir located north of Hwy. 26 between John Day and Ontario, Ore. This reservoir can go dry but is stocked with catchable and fingerling trout during wet cycles, and fishing can be very good during these periods.

The reservoir is open year-round but fishing is best in spring and fall. There is a boat ramp on the north shore and bank fishing sports around the reservoir.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Malheur River near Riverside
description:
Malheur River near the old town of Riverside, Oregon is stocked with fingerling-sized rainbow trout in the spring. These trout help to maintain a tailrace trout fishery located from Warm Springs Dam downstream to Juntura. Access is from Juntura on US Hwy 20, approximately 19 miles south on Juntura-Riverside Road.

Fish species: rainbow trout

description:

The pond is surrounded by pine trees, and behind an earthen dam across the pond is an additional 40 acres of lilly pad water. Amenities are limited, consisting of a couple of port-a-potties and some picnic tables.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, bluegill

name: Mill Flat Reservoir
description:
This is 2-acre pond located on the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) about 15 miles northwest of Lakeview, Oregon. It can be accessed by the Cox Creek road with passenger vehicles. The reservoir is stocked annually with fingerling rainbow trout that have later been caught in lengths up to 13”. This is great reservoir for people learning to fly fish because there are no trees or bushes to snag on back casts. It does become weedy during the summer, but a small section of open water remains along the dam.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Miller Lake
description:
This is a 600-acre lake located on the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) approximately 12 miles west of Hwy. 97 and Chemult, Oregon. This lake is stocked annually with rainbow trout but is better known as one of the best brown trout fisheries in Oregon. Brown trout are stocked at 6 inches. Some natural production occurs in the tributaries of the lake. Browns are generally caught by trolling in the deeper water or fishing along the shoreline in the evening. Brown trout fishing is best in November near Evening Creek or near the outlet at Miller Creek. Kokanee are also available but typically are less than eight inches in length and very rare in the catch.

Digit Point Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/fremont-winema/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=59715&actid=29) offers a full array of activities, including camping, boating, swimming, fishing and hiking. Amenities include piped water, flush toilets, RV dump station and swimming beach.

Abundant populations of small brook trout and brown trout can be found in Miller Creek at the outlet of Miller Lake. This is a great option for youths if fishing is slow in Miller Lake. Bait is allowed. Mosquitoes are very bad in the summer.

Miller Lake lamprey were reintroduced into Miller Lake in 2010. ODFW would appreciate any reports of lamprey on fish or circular wounds that might be attributed to lamprey paratism.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, kokanee, Miller Lake lamprey

name: Ochoco Reservoir
description:
This is a 1,100-acre reservoir located approximately 5 miles east of Prineville, Oregon on Hwy 26. This reservoir is open year-around and is stocked with sub-legal rainbow trout, which later reach sizes of 12-15 inches. There are also wild redband trout available from natural production in tributary streams. Black crappie fishing is excellent during the summer months.

Boats are allowed on this reservoir and can launch from a ramp at Ochoco Lake Campground (http://www.ccprd.org/OchocoLake.php), which has 22 self-contained camping spaces available April 1 through spproximately October 31 each year, depending on weather. Facilities include picnic tables, fire pits, restrooms and fish cleaning station.

Fish species: rainbow trout, black crappie, smallmouth bass, brown bullhead.



ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/central-zone/)
Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)
Fishing Regulations (http://www.eregulations.com/oregon/fishing/)

name: Odell Lake
description:
This is a 3,300-acre natural mountain lake located on the east side of Willamette Pass approximately 70 miles southeast of Eugene, Oregon just off Hwy 58.

Fed by a numerous small creeks and springs, this lake supports good populations of kokanee, lake trout and redband trout.The lake also supports a small population of bull trout, which are protected and must be released unharmed.

There are several improved boat ramps around the lake, and trolling with downriggers, leaded line and flashers are popular fishing techniques, as is casting from shore. Kokanee are abundant and average 11-13 inches while lake trout over 10 pounds are common.

Camping is available at Princess Creek (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39088&actid=29), Trapper Creek (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39120&actid=29) and Sunset Cove (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=39112&actid=29) campgrounds, which are managed by the U.S. Forest Service. Overnight accomodations are available Shelter Cove Resort (http://www.sheltercoveresort.com/) and Odell Lake Lodge and Resort (http://odelllakeresort.com/), which offer cabin rentals, supplies, boats and tackle.

Fish species: Kokanee, lake trout, redband trout, bull trout.

name: Olalla Creek Reservoir
description:
Olalla Creek Reservoir is a 120-acre reservoir on Olalla Creek about a mile north of Toledo, Ore. The reservoir reaches a maximum depth of about 65 feet near Olalla dam. The shoreline and much of the surrounding landscape is owned by Georgia Pacific Corporation and managed as commercial timberland. The company allows public access to the water, and small boats without motors may be launched.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout

Trout Stocking Schedule - https://myodfw.com/fishing/species/trout/stocking-schedule?field_zone_value=All&field_waterbody_name_value_1=OLALLA+CR+RESERVOIR&field_planned_stocking_date_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-01-01&field_planned_stocking_date_end_value%5Bdate%5D=2019-12-31

name: Olallie Lake
description:
This is the largest of more than 200 lakes within the Olallie Lake Scenic Area. Located on the southern edge of the Mt. Hood National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/mthood) it is a popular summer recreational destination for people from Portland and Salem, Ore.

There are three campgrounds and a rustic cabin resort on this lake as well as a hiking trail that encircles the perimeter. Yurts, cabins, and boat rentals are available at Olallie Lake Resort (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/mthood/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=52978&actid=29). There is a boat ramp at Peninsula Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/mthood/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=52994&actid=29) on the southwest shore of the lake. Camping is also available at Olallie Meadows Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/mthood/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=52982&actid=29) and Paul Dennis Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/mthood/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=52990&actid=29). Olallie Lake is also a popular jumping off point for backpackers who want to fish the surrounding high lakes or access the Pacific Crest Trail (http://www.fs.usda.gov/pct/).

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Overton Pond
description:
This small reservoir is located on the Fremont-Wnema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) about 20 miles northeast of Lakeview. This reservoir can be accessed using Forest Service Road #3615 then turning onto Forest Service Road #3624 when the pavement ends (about 15 miles off of Hwy 140). The reservoir is stocked with fingerling rainbow trout annually and they have been caught to 12”. Milfoil covers the pond from June to September but a hole can open when the wind blows from the right direction.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Paiute Reservoir
description:
This is an irrigation reservoir located about 20 miles east of Adel, Oregon approximately one mile south of Hwy 140. The road to access the reservoir is located between milepost 49 and 50 and has a stop sign. Some years this reservoir is full while other years it is dry. The reservoir is stocked with rainbow trout and Lahontan cutthroat trout when there is water. The water is muddy and anglers should target the margins for better success. Ice fishing is available at this reservoir some years. Call the Bureau of Land Management's Lakeview Field Office (http://www.blm.gov/or/districts/lakeview/notesdirectory.php) to verify that water is in the reservoir.

Fish species: rainbow trout, Lahontan cutthroat trout

name: Paulina Lake
description:
This is a 1,520-acre lake located within the Newberry Volcanic National Monument (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recarea/?recid=66159), about 25 miles south of Bend, Oregon. The lake is situated in the caldera of the Newberry volcano at an elevation of 6,350 ft. It has a maximum depth of 250 ft. and is open to motorized boats, which can launch from ramps at Little Crater Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38400&actid=29) and Paulina Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/camping-cabins/recarea/?recid=38442&actid=29). The Paulina Lake Lodge offers lodging, meals and supplies.

Paulina provides great opportunity for brown trout, rainbow trout and kokanee. The state record brown trout was caught here.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, kokanee

name: Pine Hollow Reservoir
description:
This is a 240-acre irrigation reservoir located approximately 39 miles south of The Dalles, Oregon. The lake is surrounded by private homes but fishing access has been preserved through a 10-foot easment around the reservoir purchased by ODFW, although public bank fishing is limited to the area west of the south shore boat ramp and north and south of the east shore ramp.

There are two public boat ramps and a privately operated resort on the east shore. Fishing is allowed year-round, although the lake sometimes freezes over during the winter.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, brown bullhead, bluegill

name: Prineville Reservoir
description:
This is a 3,000-acre reservoir located approximately 17 miles southeast of Prineville. This reservoir was created for irrigation and flood control by damming the Crooked River behind Arthur R. Bowman Dam. It is situated at an elevation of 3,200 ft. and reaches depths of up to 120 ft.

The reservoir is open to motorized boats and supports a diverse population of fish, including rainbow trout, black crappie and smallmouth and largemouth bass. The reservoir is stocked annually with sublegal rainbow trout. A boat ramp, boot moorage, picnic areas are available at Prineville Reservoir State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_34.php). Lodging and supplies are available at the Prineville Reservoir Resort.

Fish species: rainbow trout, smallmouth bass, largemouth bass, black crappie, brown bullhead

name: Quartzville Creek - Boulder Cr. Bridge
description:
Quartzville Creek is a wild and scenic river extending 12 miles upstream from Green Peter Reservoir to the Willamette National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/willamette) boundry. It offers a variety of recreational opportunities such as gold panning, fishing, camping and high-flow kayaking. The Quartzville Back Country Byway (http://www.blm.gov/or/resources/recreation/files/brochures/quartzville_110119.pdf) parallels the winding river, offering views of old-growth forests and wildlife and providing easy access to developed and undeveloped picnic and camping sites.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout

name: Sherlock Gulch Reservoir
description:
This is a 10-acre reservoir located within the Bureau of Land Management's Lakeview, Oregon District (http://www.blm.gov/or/districts/lakeview/index.php). The reservoir is stocked with fingerling rainbow trout annually and have been caught up to 12”. The reservoir is muddy all year and anglers should target areas along the margin of the reservoir to catch fish. A fun side trip would be to stop by the public sunstone collection area or one of the private companies that specialize in hunting for sunstones, Oregon’s state gem.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Shevlin Pond
description:
Shevlin Pond is a half-acre pond located among aspen trees in beautiful Shevlin Park (http://www.bendparksandrec.org/.docs/pg/10071) four miles west of Bend. It is open year-round but often freezes in the winter. There is a casting dock and good bank access around the pond making this is a great place to teach inexperienced anglers how to fish.

Fishing here is restricted to juvenile anglers up to 17 years old. The daily limit bag is two trout with an 8-inch minimum length. The pond is stocked with rainbow trout from April to August, though fishing can be good through November.

Facilities include restrooms and a wheelchair accessible bridge and dock. Shevlin Park also has hiking and mountain biking trails with scenic picnic areas throughout the park.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Siltcoos Lake
description:
Siltcoos Lake, at 3,164 acres in size, is the largest lake on the central Oregon Coast. The lake is very popular for boating and fishing. The shoreline tends to be brushy and mostly in private ownership. For this reason, fishing from boats is prefered by most anglers.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, largemouth bass, yellow perch, crappie, bluegill, bullhead

name: Simtustus Lake
description:
This is a deep reservoir formed by impoundment of the Deschutes River behind Pelton Dam about 6 miles west of Madras.Ore.. It is bordered by the Warm Springs Indian Reservation on the western shoreline. The dam backs up about 7 miles of the river, which is accessible almost exclusively by boat.

The reservoir is open to fishing from late April through October. It is stocked with legal-sized rainbow trout before opening weekend and through the summer. A tribal fishing permit is required in addition to a state fishing license.

There is a store, boat ramp and campground at Pelton Park. Boats are subject to a 10 m.p.h. speed limit on much of the reservoir, and boat landings on the west shore are prohibited, except at Indian Park.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, kokanee, largemouth bass, bull trout

name: Smith Reservoir
description:
Reservoir on the Willamette National Forest appr. 70 miles east of Springfield, Ore., This reservoir spans 170 acres and is part of the Carmen-Smith Hydroelectric Project, owned by the Eugene Water & Electric Board. Smith complex offers camping, picknicking and a boat launch maintained by the U.S. Forest Service.
The Eugene Water & Electric Board is conducting a five-year construction project to retrofit, refurbish and upgrade capital equipment at its Carmen-Smith Hydroelectric Project. The work is being conducted in anticipation of a new federal operating license for the project.

The capital construction projects planned for the 2017-2021 time frame will create significant public access constraints due primarily to safety concerns. In order to keep the public and construction personnel safe during the five-year project, EWEB and the Forest Service agreed to close access to Forest Road 730 at the Powerhouse. The closure will deny public access to Trail Bridge Campground, Smith Reservoir and Lake’s End Campground. The closure of the road to the public will begin in March 2017 and continue through 2021.
Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, eastern brook trout


More information (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/willamette/recreation/recarea/?recid=4400)

name: South Yamhill River
description:
The South Fork Yamhill from its confluence with the North Yamhill near McMinnville, upstream about 20 miles to Rock Creek near Grand Ronde is stocked with rainbow trout.

Trout are released in multiple locations between Gold Creek Road Bridge and Willamina.Yamhill River Road runs parallel to much of this section and provides adequate turnouts and parking at several locations near the river. The remaining 15 miles of river open to trout fishing has some public access but also meanders across private lands. ODFW reminds anglers to be aware of and respectful toward private property rights along the river.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout

description:
This is a 3-acre lake located on a historic mill site within the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/rogue-siskiyou/). It has three ADA accessible fishing platforms and a surfaced accessible trail in a primitive setting appr. 21 miles southwest of Grants Pass, Ore. There is an accessible restroom and small campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/rogue-siskiyou/recreation/recarea/?recid=70678). There is a small pebble beach.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout

name: Sparks Lake
description:
This is a 400-acre lake located about 25 miles west of Bend. It is a large, shallow, trout lake situated on the northwest edge of Mount Bachelor. It is the first of the high lakes visible from the Cascade Lakes Highway west of Bend and offers views of South Sister, Mount Bachelor, and Broken Top. Prior to 1997, Sparks Lake was a brook trout fishery and, although brookies are still available, the lake's featured species is now cutthroat trout. Restricted to fly angling only.

Motorized and non-motorized boats are allowed on this lake. However, fishing from a motorized boat is prohibited. Due to the spectacular sceneray and proximity to Bend, Sparks Lake is very popular with canoeists and kayakers..

Fish species: cutthroat trout, brook trout

name: Spaulding Reservoir
description:
This is a 20-acre reservoir located 30 miles southeast of Adel, Oregon within the Bureau of Land Management's Lakeview, Oregon District (http://www.blm.gov/or/districts/lakeview/index.php). A brown recreation sign is located on highway 140 at the turn-off for the reservoir. Another sign is located 11 miles down the dirt road. The reservoir is stocked with fingerling rainbow trout when water is present. Fish are currently about 10” and readily bite dark colored flies. Anglers should not attempt to drive to the reservoir if the road is muddy.

Fish species: rainbow trout

description:

name: Suttle Lake
description:
This is a 240-acre lake located within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) about 14 miles northwest of Sisters, Oregon, just off Hwy 20.

In addition to fishing, this is a multi-use lake offers other recreational opportunities including, swimming, boating and water skiing. There are four boat ramps on the south shore of the lake, and U.S. Forest Service campgrounds on the northeast and southeast ends of the lake. The Lodge at Suttle Lake (http://thelodgeatsuttlelake.com/) offers lodging, cabin rentals, a restaurant and a marina with boat rentals and moorage.

Suttle Lake supports populations of kokanee, brown and small numbers of rainbow trout . Kokanee is the most popular fishery and takes place from May through August. Trophy size brown trout are also available

Fish species: Kokanee, brown trout, rainbow trout

description:

name: Tenmile Lake
description:
Two large lakes on the Oregon coast next to the town of Lakeside in Coos County. The lakes have 170 miles of shoreline, a surface area of 2,723 acres and an average depth of about 15 feet. They have numerous bays, arms and fingers that hold a variety of trout and warmwater fish species. These lakes are considered one of Oregon's premier warmwater fisheries. The lake is located just minutes from the Oregon Dunes National Recreation Area, (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c5/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72DTgBAjAwgAykeaxcN4jhYG_h4eYX5hPgYwefy6w0H24dcPNgEHcDTQ9_PIz03VL8iNMMgycVQEAObEnGA!/dl3/d3/L2dJQSEvUUt3QS9ZQnZ3LzZfME80MEkxVkFCOTBFMktTNUJIMjAwMDAwMDA!/?navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&recid=42465&actid=&navid=110000000000000&pnavid=&ss=110612&position=&ttype=recarea&pname=Siuslaw%20National%20Forest%20-%20Oregon%20Dunes%20National%20Recreation%20Area) which has numerous campgrounds, hiking trails, and other recreational amenities.

Amenities: county park, two boat ramps, three marinas

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, largemouth bass, crappie, bluegill, yellow perch, brown bullhead

description:

name: Thompson Valley Reservoir
description:
Thisis a 2500-acre reservoir in a forested area of the Fremont-Winema NationalForest about 60 miles northeast of Klamath Falls, Oregon. This reservoir isopen year-around, and ice fishing is popular when snow allows access. Campingis available at two campgrounds (Thompson Reservoir and East Bay) and on thereservoir, and each has a boat ramp. The ramp at Thompson Valley Campground isgood for small boats, while the ramp at East Bay campground is better forlarger boats.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, chub

name: Three Creeks Lake
description:
This is a 28-acre lake in the Deschutes National Forest. It is approximately 20 miles south of Sisters. Situated at an elevation of 6,500 feet in a beautiful cirque below Tam McCarthur Rim, this lake is often snowbound until May or June. Camping is available on the north shore of the lake at Driftwood Campground. Angling is excellent via trolling from a boat or canoe. Fly angling with float tubes is also popular.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brook trout

name: Timothy Lake - Gone Creek Campground
description:
Timothy Lake is one of the most popular family camping and fishing destinations in the Mt. Hood National Forest. The lake's south shore features four developed campgrounds and boat ramps. Three smaller, less developed campgrounds are found in the north. A trail system for hikers, mountain bikers and equestrians circles the lake. The Pacific Crest National Scenic Trail also traverses the area along the east side of the lake. Motorboats are allowed on Timothy Lake, although a 10 m.p.h. speed limit is in place.
Fish species: rainbow trout, kokanee, eastern brook, cutthroat trout, crayfish. Anglers are asked to identify their catch and are encouraged to release native cutthroat trout unharmed.
Lat./Long. Coordinates: 45.113633,-121.773577

name: Town Lake
description:
This is a 9-acre impoundement located near the community of Woods, Ore. Most of the lake is surrounded by private land but public access is available along the west shore near the boat ramp, and includes the boat ramp itself. The bank to the lake is steep and brushy. An angling dock is located on the west side of the lake and is accessible by steep, unimproved trails that must be used with extreme caution.

name: Twin Lake - North
description:
This is a 130-acre lake located within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) about 45 miles southwest of Bend, Oregon. Open year round, it provides a fishery for 9 to 11-inch rainbow trout. It is a great family setting and provides good shelter from the wind. The lake offers good boat fishing, although use of motors is prohibited.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Twin Lake - South
description:
This is a 99-acre natural lake located within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) about 60 miles southwest of Bend, Oregon off of the Cascade Lakes Highway.

Consistent stocking of fingerlings and legal-size rainbow trout has resulted in an excellent fishery. Most anglers’ fish from boats and use bait. Depths of 15 to 30 feet are the best producers. Trolling slowly with a flasher and apiece of worm or egg or with a very small lure are productive methods throughout the season. Still fishing with eggs, worms, and a marshmallow or power bait far enough off the bottom to stay out of the weeds is also a popular method.

South Twin Lake provides good shoreline access for both kids and adults. It provides a sheltered fishing venue when early season cold temperatures and wind become a problem on larger water bodies.

Only non-motorized boats are allowed on this lake

Camping is available at South Twin Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/recarea/?recid=39002). The South Twin Resort offers lodging, accommodations and supplies.

Fish species: rainbow trout

Only the larger lake is stocked with fingerling "FishWich" Rainbow Trout. There is a primitive campground in the area.

Fish species: brook trout, Rainbow

name: Vee Lake
description:
This 14-acre reservoir is located on the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) about 25 miles northeast of Lakeview. This reservoir can be accessed using NF Road #3615 then turning onto NF Road #3616 follow the signs to the Vee Lake trailhead. The reservoir is stocked with fingerling, catchable, and trophy rainbow trout annually and they have been caught to 14”. There are two campsites and a pit toilet and boat ramp for small boats or float tubes. Vegetation can be an issue for bank anglers during the summer. This is a great location to fish and view Hart Mountain National Antelope Refuge (http://www.fws.gov/sheldonhartmtn/Hart/index.html) or watch the stars during the summer. Access is limited to snowmobiles during the winter.

Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Vernonia Pond
description:
Located at the north end of Vernonia, Ore., on Hwy 47, this waterbody was once the mill pond of the Oregon-American Lumber Company.
Amenities: Primitive/walk-in camping area with fire pits, restrooms, drinking water, boat ramp (gas powered motors are not allowed), ADA wheelchair accessible fishing docks and restrooms, bank fishing access, and children’s playground area. There is an on-site bait shop during the spring and summer months. The Banks-Vernonia bike trail starts and ends at this site. Fishing is best in spring, as warmer temperatures promote growth of aquatic vegetation. The city of Vernonia owns this pond and charges $5 to park at the site.

Fish species: rainbow trout, largemouth bass, crappie, bluegill, brown bullhead, surplus hatchery steelhead. description:
Walter Wirth Lake is a 20-acre pond located within the City of Salem's Cascades Gateway Park. The lake is named after Walter L. Wirth, a park superintendent for the City of Salem Parks Department from 1954-1958.
Mill Stream, which runs through the park, has become a spawning area for fall Chinook salmon.

description:
This is a 19-acre lake located on within the Ochoco National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) approxiamtely 31 miles northeast of Prineville. This lake is open the entire year and is stocked prior to Memorial Day weekend and throughout the summer, with holdover trout reaching 16-18 inches. Snow limits access during the winter months.Boats are allowed but are restricted to electric motors. Camping is available at Walton Lake Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/recarea/centraloregon/recreation/recarea/?recid=38766), which has a boat ramp, picnic tables, vault toilets and drinking water.

Fish species: rainbow trout


ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/central-zone/)
Trout Stocking Schedule (http://www.dfw.state.or.us/resources/fishing/trout_stocking_schedules/)

name: Warm Springs Reservoir
description:
This is a 4,200-acre irrigation reservoir located about 49 miles east of Burns, Oregon via U.S.Hwy 20, then 14 miles south on a dirt road to the boat ramp. This reservoir is managed primarily for warmwater fish, but a few fingerling-sized rainbow trout are stocked in the spring.

Fish species: rainbow trout, warmwater fish

name: Warner Lakes
description:
This is a chain of lakes aligned from north to south throughout the Warner Basin of southeast Oregon. These lakes are located near the small towns of Adel, Oregon and Plush, Oregon. Crump and Hart Lakes usually have water, but many of the lakes to the north become dry for multiple years. These lakes have crappie and brown bullhead with the occasional redband trout. These lakes produce state record crappie when water persists for a few years. A campground with pit toilets is available at the ‘CCC camp’ on Hart Mountain National Antelope Refuge (http://www.fws.gov/sheldonhartmtn/Hart/index.html).

Fish species: rainbow trout

ODFW Recreation Report & regulation updates (http://myodfw.com/recreation-report/fishing-report/southeast-zone/)

name: Waverly Lake
description:
Waverly Lake is is located in an Albany City Park with restrooms and picnic facilities. In the summer months, paddle boats are available for rent.Other recreational activities are available nearby including paved bike trails, hiking and wildlife viewing. Waverly Lake offers excellent bank fishing opportunities and also has good access for disabled anglers, including a dock. There is a paved trail around the entire lake.

Fish species: rainbow trout, blue catfish, brown trout, crappie, largemouth bass

name: Wickiup Reservoir
description:
This is a 11,200-acre reservoir within the Deschutes National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/centraloregon) created by the impoundment of the upper Deschutes River behind Wickiup Dam, approximately 40 miles southwest of Bend. This is one of Oregon's largest and most productive fisheries, offering self-sustaining populations of kokanee, brown trout, and rainbow trout. Both kokanee (up to 20 inches) and brown trout (up to 15 pounds) grow quite large here.

This is primarily a boat fishery, although casting from shore is also popular. Depths range from 20 to 65 feet. Good boat ramps are available at Gull Point (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c5/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTSw8jAwgAykeaxcN4jhYG_h4eYX5hPgYwefy6w0H24dcPNgEHcDTQ9_PIz03VL8iNMMgycVQEAIzTHkw!/dl3/d3/L2dJQSEvUUt3QS9ZQnZ3LzZfME80MEkxVkFCOTBFMktTNUJIMjAwMDAwMDA!/?ss=110601&ttype=recarea&recid=38874&actid=29&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110130000000000&pnavid=110000000000000&cid=FSE_003705&pname=Central+Oregon+-+Gull+Point+Campground) and North Wickiup campgrounds at the mouth of the Deschutes arm, at West South Twin Campground (http://www.fs.usda.gov/wps/portal/fsinternet/!ut/p/c4/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os3gDfxMDT8MwRydLA1cj72BTJw8jAwjQL8h2VAQAzHJMsQ!!?ss=110601&ttype=recarea&recid=39030&actid=31&navtype=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&position=BROWSEBYSUBJECT&navid=110000000000000&pnavid=&cid=null&pname=Central+Oregon+-+West+South+Twin+Campground) on the lower Deschutes Arm, Reservoir Campground on the southwest shore, and Wickiup Butte Campground on the southeast shore.

Fish species: rainbow trout, brown trout, kokanee, whitefish, largemouth bass

name: Willow Lake
description:
Willow Lake is a popular southern Oregon reservoir which stores the waters of Willow Creek, a tributary of the South Fork of Big Butte Creek. The 52-foot high, earthfill dam was completed in 1952 by the Medford Water Commission to store water as part of their municipal water system. The lake is located at the base of Mt. McLoughlin on land owned by Jackson County. It is located approximately 7.5 miles east of Butte Falls and 43 miles northeast of Medfore, Ore. Jackson County has designated the surrounding 927 acres as a recreational area, which provides a number of amenities, including campsites, RV sites, cabins, picnic area, restrooms, and boat ramp.

Amenities: Willow Lake County Park
(http://www.co.jackson.or.us/Page.asp?NavID=3514)
Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, kokanee, largemouth bass, black crappie, brown bullhead

name: Withers Lake
description:
This is a 5-acre lake located on the Fremont-Winema National Forest (http://www.fs.usda.gov/fremont-winema) between Summer Lake and Paisley. Anglers can access the trail from the Government Harvey Pass road coming from Summer Lake or via the High Line road coming from Paisley. The lake is stocked with brown trout annually and has naturally produced brook trout. Brown trout range from 8”-14” with a few larger fish. This is a beautiful lake to float tube during the summer or fall. There is a 100-yard walk from the parking area to the lake. A pit toilet located at the parking area.


Fish species: rainbow trout

name: Woahink Lake
description:
Woahink Lake is 820 acres in size and, at 72 feet deep, is the deepest lake on the central Oregon Coast. Much of the shoreline is in private residential ownership. About 15 percent lies within Honeyman State Park (http://www.oregonstateparks.org/park_134.php), which provides ample access, including a boat ramp, large day-use area and covered picnic shelters.

Fish species: rainbow trout, cutthroat trout, largemouth bass, yellow perch
